quote: Oh, you didn't qualify for it?
stewart_read: Carter surfaces the prior-disqualification concern but doesn't resolve it — he asks a clarifying question, gets an uncertain answer ('I'm not quite sure'), and then pivots to a pitch. The unresolved 'why didn't I qualify' is the customer's primary anxiety and it never gets addressed. Reactive read: Carter heard the concern and moved past it. Strategic read: Carter was deferring resolution to the closer. The reactive read is more supported — Carter's next move is a generic pitch, not a setup for a later resolution.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter was intentionally deferring the disqualification question to the closer, where it can be properly addressed with the full qualification toolkit.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask Carter: 'What did you do with the disqualification concern?' The correct move here is a brief reframe — 'We haven't actually met with you yet, so whatever happened before wasn't with us. Let's start fresh and see what you actually qualify for.' This clears the anxiety and resets the customer's frame before the pitch.

quote: there is a tax credit in place right now that's gonna be leaving at the end of the year.
stewart_read: Carter deploys the tax-credit urgency lever before he knows whether the customer qualifies for it. The customer's fiance is the title-holder and the credit qualifier hasn't been confirmed yet. Using urgency on a customer who may not qualify for the incentive being cited is a credibility risk — if the closer later has to walk back the tax credit, the handoff is damaged. This is a reactive move: Carter reached for the nearest urgency tool without checking whether it applied.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter was using the tax credit as a general urgency frame, not a specific promise, and intended to qualify it later in the call.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag the sequencing: urgency levers should follow qualification, not precede it. Carter confirmed credit qualification at 03:51 — that's the right order. Here he inverted it. The coaching note from the codex on tax credit disclosure ('We can no longer guarantee the Tax Credit') makes this sequencing gap more consequential.

quote: their upfront cost is just gonna be eliminated completely, and then we'd swap it from your utility bill. Looks like from what I see, it's your West Southside Electric. So we'd swap that, your expensive bill, to a cheaper fixated solar bill.
stewart_read: Carter explains the financial mechanism of solar — upfront cost elimination, bill swap — before the customer asked. This is setter-scope creep: the setter's job is to create curiosity and schedule the closer, not to explain how the product works financially. The explanation is accurate but premature. It also partially answers the payment question Quanisha asks at 04:54, which means Carter gave away closer-tier content for free and then had to repeat it when she asked anyway.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter was using the bill-swap framing as a curiosity hook, not a full product explanation, and kept it brief enough to stay within setter scope.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to illustrate the setter_scope_discipline principle: 'What's the minimum you need to say to make her curious enough to show up?' The bill-swap framing is the right instinct, but the mechanism explanation belongs to the closer. Suggested redirect: 'The specialist will show you exactly how the numbers work for your home — that's the whole point of the appointment.'

quote: my fiance name is on it. Mine is not at at this time.
stewart_read: Quanisha discloses that her fiance is the title-holder, not her. Carter acknowledges this ('Okay. So your fiance is the owner, though') and moves on without addressing the scheduling implication. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the title-holder must be on the appointment. Carter never asks whether the fiance will be available, never confirms the fiance's name, and never anchors the appointment around both of them being present. The call ends with a soft scheduling ask directed at Quanisha alone ('are you both gonna be around on Monday'), which is the only gesture toward fiance inclusion — and it's too late and too soft.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter's 'are you both gonna be around on Monday' at 05:48 was a deliberate attempt to include the fiance, and the soft close was intentional to avoid pressure.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Carter through the spouse_decision protocol directly. The moment at 02:37 is where the fiance inclusion should have been locked: 'Since he's on the title, we'll need him on the call too — what time works for both of you?' The call ends without the fiance confirmed for the appointment, which makes any future sit structurally vulnerable.

stewart_read: Quanisha confirms her fiance is active duty or retired military. Carter's response is 'Just make sure to bring that up in the appointment' — he flags it but doesn't use it as a scheduling anchor or a rapport moment. Military status is a meaningful qualifier signal: it confirms the fiance is a real person with a real identity, it may unlock program eligibility, and it's a natural bridge to fiance inclusion ('Since he's served, he'll want to be part of this conversation — let's make sure he's on the call'). Carter treats it as a checkbox rather than a lever.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter correctly scoped military status as a closer-tier detail and appropriately deferred it.
coaching_implication: Manager should show Carter how to use the military disclosure as a fiance-inclusion anchor. The setter-appropriate use is narrow: 'Since he's the one on the title and he's served, let's make sure he's on the appointment — what time works for both of you?' The closer-tier uses (program eligibility, trust signal) belong in the handoff brief, not the setter coaching note.

quote: Wait. If we were to get a food for it, is this something that can be paid monthly or, upfront complete you know, one the whole thing?
stewart_read: This is the call's peak engagement moment. Quanisha interrupts the bill-collection ask to ask an unprompted financial question — she's mentally modeling ownership. Carter answers it adequately (most people choose monthly, you could pay upfront), but he doesn't anchor on it. The correct move here is to use her curiosity as a scheduling lever: 'That's exactly the kind of question the specialist will walk through with you — the monthly vs. upfront math for your specific home. That's the whole point of the appointment.' Instead, Carter answers the question and moves back to bill collection, letting the peak engagement moment dissipate.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter answered the question to maintain momentum and avoid scope creep, which is a defensible choice.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to illustrate the curiosity_over_features principle: when the customer asks a financial question unprompted, the right move is to redirect to the appointment, not answer the question. Carter answered it and moved on — he gave away the closer's best hook for free. The coaching question: 'What would have happened if you'd said the specialist will show you the exact monthly vs. upfront breakdown for your home?'

quote: Just send me that utility bill as soon as you can. And then if you could also just let me know a time that works, that'd be great.
stewart_read: Carter exits with two open-ended asks and no locked appointment. The button_up protocol requires a confirmed datetime, a one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving anchor, and an 'is that fair' handshake. None of these elements are present. The call ends as a soft callback — 'let me know a time' — which has materially higher no-show risk than a locked sit. Per the setter comp model, this call does not pay until it sits, and a soft callback is the most fragile path to a sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter was respecting the customer's stated schedule uncertainty ('so many things come up sometimes all at once') and chose not to pressure a locked time.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Carter through the button_up protocol and the comp model implication: a soft callback doesn't pay. The customer's schedule uncertainty is real, but the correct response is to offer two specific options ('Does Monday at 10 or Tuesday at 2 work better for both of you?') rather than an open ask. The 'is that fair' handshake is the verbal commitment that predicts show rate — Carter skipped it entirely.
